response: After the consultation, Dr. Shelby determines next steps based on medical necessity. This may include scheduling a procedure or ordering additional testing such as labs or imaging.
Dr. Shelby specializes in reflux disease (GERD), peptic ulcer disease, inflammatory bowel disease, gastrointestinal cancers, pancreatic disorders, and biliary disorders.
Banding is typically scheduled on procedure days. Our team can offer the next available slot.
Correct—banding typically requires no prep and no anesthesia, and you can usually drive yourself.
Banding/procedure visits are typically Suite 100. If unsure on arrival, call the office and we’ll guide you to the correct door.
Re-banding typically has no prep and no anesthesia. It’s quick, and you can usually drive yourself.
Hemorrhoid banding usually has no prep and no anesthesia, and you can typically drive yourself. It’s a quick visit.
If hemorrhoids don’t improve with conservative care or banding, Dr. Shelby may refer you to a colorectal/hemorrhoid surgeon for evaluation.
Hemorrhoids can improve with conservative care. Banding helps many patients; surgery is more definitive but is handled by a surgeon if needed.
A sitz bath basin with lukewarm water and Epsom salt can help hemorrhoids seal and stop bleeding. Avoid constipation and straining.
If you have significant bloating/slow colon, high fiber can worsen symptoms. Dr. Shelby may recommend Miralax and/or prescription bowel regulators instead.
For severe constipation, Dr. Shelby may use a colonoscopy-style flush (jug prep) as a reset, then maintenance with a bowel regulator or Miralax.
Often the driver only needs to pick you up after the procedure (they don’t necessarily need to stay). We can call them when you’re ready.
Because anesthesia is used, you can’t drive the rest of the day. You need someone to pick you up afterward.
Align is a commonly recommended probiotic to help repopulate gut flora after infections/antibiotics. Use as directed and follow up if symptoms persist.
HPV-related warts can occur and may need colorectal evaluation. If you have concerns, schedule follow-up so we can discuss privately and plan next steps.
Some anal lesions can mimic hemorrhoids. If there’s uncertainty, Dr. Shelby may biopsy and refer to a colorectal surgeon if HPV/wart is suspected.
For travel-related constipation, a small amount of Miralax at night can help keep you regular. Discuss dosing with your doctor.
Capsule endoscopy has its own prep timing and restrictions. If scheduled, we’ll send the specific instructions (including no MRI until it passes).
Borderline/weak positive tests may not be definitive. If symptoms persist, confirmation can be done by endoscopy/biopsy; otherwise dietary moderation may be enough.
If you were referred to a colorectal surgeon, please call the number provided in your referral message and schedule evaluation. We can resend referral details if needed.
If needed, we can refer to a colorectal/rectal surgeon for hemorrhoids, warts/HPV lesions, or leakage evaluation.
If a hernia may be contributing to constipation, CT imaging can help clarify anatomy. Call us to coordinate an order if recommended.
Keeping bowel movements regular helps reduce constipation-related flares. Dr. Shelby may prescribe a bowel regulator; adjust dose if too strong.
FibroSure-type blood tests can estimate fatty liver severity and fibrosis risk. We can place the order (often LabCorp) and review results at follow-up.
Some liver blood tests (like FibroSure) often do not require fasting. Follow the lab instructions when ordered.
If an imaging order is needed (CT/ultrasound), we can email it or you can pick up a paper order at the office during business hours.
GLP-1s can be effective for weight/metabolic control, but can worsen constipation. Discuss prescribing with endocrinology/PCP; GI can advise about side effects.
Some patients tolerate different GLP-1s differently. Discuss with your prescribing doctor; GI can advise about constipation/diarrhea side effects.
In select young/inpatient anemia cases, imaging (upper GI/small bowel follow-through) may be considered before endoscopy/colonoscopy depending on clinical picture.
MRCP orders are often placed with and without contrast depending on protocol. We can confirm your order details when placing it.
Capsule endoscopy has special restrictions (no MRI until capsule passes; no flying next day per some protocols). Confirm your exact instructions when scheduled.
We do not typically perform anoscopy as an office procedure. Dr. Shelby can evaluate and recommend appropriate testing or referral.
If you have GI irritation/loose stool after a procedure, avoiding dairy temporarily can help. Follow up if symptoms persist.
If ulcer is suspected without clear imaging findings, Dr. Shelby may treat with pantoprazole + Carafate and only scope if symptoms persist.
Very small pancreatic cysts can be monitored; if imaging is reassuring, Dr. Shelby may recommend routine surveillance as appropriate.
If you have strong family history of pancreatic cancer, the doctor may recommend surveillance imaging (such as MRCP) on an interval. Call to coordinate orders.
Pregnancy/postpartum can increase liver fat. Dr. Shelby may order a FibroSure-type blood test to quantify fat/fibrosis risk and guide follow-up.
Colonoscopy and endoscopy can be done in one setting under one anesthesia, which is often easier than separate procedures.
Most patients are at the facility about an hour (sometimes longer depending on recovery).
Plan to be at the surgery center about 1 hour total (sometimes up to 1–1.5 hours depending on recovery).
Persistent leakage can be related to hemorrhoids or other anorectal issues. Dr. Shelby may refer you to a colorectal surgeon to evaluate for hemorrhoids/fistula.
Anal HPV lesions can carry cancer risk, so referral to a colorectal surgeon for evaluation/removal vs monitoring is standard.
Food allergy blood tests can have false positives. If symptoms correlate with a specific food, avoiding that trigger is reasonable; discuss confirmation testing if needed.
If symptoms persist and pancreatic enzymes aren’t fully effective, SIBO breath testing may be considered—often after any acute illness resolves.
Upper abdominal pain can be GI or musculoskeletal. If CT is unrevealing, Dr. Shelby may trial ulcer/reflux therapy and scope if not improving.
Tucks pads and Vaseline can help for irritation/burning. If symptoms persist or worsen, schedule an exam.
Benign liver cysts are often followed with periodic ultrasound (e.g., annually). Confirm your interval with Dr. Shelby.
If you’re due for annual ultrasound follow-up, call us near the target month and we can place the imaging order.
Weight loss and improved metabolic control can reduce fatty liver/MASH risk. Dr. Shelby may recommend periodic lab follow-up and lifestyle maintenance.
If symptoms correlate with wheat, moderating intake can help. If severe or persistent, confirmation can be discussed with your GI specialist.
A short Miralax trial (e.g., 1 capful nightly up to 3 days) can help assess slow colon vs other causes, based on the timing of response.
For some high-risk pancreatic family history cases, an annual MRCP surveillance order may be done (sometimes self-pay). Call the office to coordinate the order and facility.
Some viral GI infections can last weeks. Supportive measures (hydration, fiber powder, Imodium as needed) may help; follow up if not improving.
After the procedure, avoid greasy/spicy foods at first. Most people are back to normal routine by the next day.

The procedure itself is usually brief, but the total time at the center is longer for check-in, preparation, and recovery. For the best estimate for your scheduled time and procedure, please call the office.
Procedures are scheduled on a different day than the consultation because prep/fasting is required. The consult is to decide what you need and then schedule it.

Timing depends on medical need, insurance authorization (if required), and scheduling availability. Please email your question to shelbygimd@gmail.com, and my supervisor will email you the answer based on your situation.
Dr. Shelby performs upper endoscopy (EGD), colonoscopy, and ERCP. If you have questions about a specific procedure, our staff can help guide you.
Dr. Shelby performs common GI procedures such as colonoscopy and upper endoscopy (EGD). For questions about a specific procedure or indication, please call the office so we can guide you.
Based on the consultation and clinical findings, Dr. Shelby may recommend an upper endoscopy (EGD), a colonoscopy, both procedures, or other appropriate laboratory or imaging tests as needed.
Uber/Lyft is acceptable for pickup in many cases. If you’re unsure about your case, call us to confirm.
We generally recommend screening colonoscopy starting at age 45 (earlier if high-risk).
Stool tests can miss cancers/polyps. Colonoscopy is the most definitive test because it allows direct inspection and polyp removal.
Average-risk screening colonoscopy is often every 10 years, but your interval may be shorter based on polyps, symptoms, or family history.
After a precancerous polyp, repeat colonoscopy is often recommended around 3 years to check for regrowth. Confirm your interval with Dr. Shelby.
If your last colonoscopy was normal/low-risk, the typical interval can be about 5 years (sometimes longer). We can confirm your specific recommendation in your chart.
Whether to continue screening depends on overall health and life expectancy, not a strict age cutoff. Dr. Shelby will weigh benefits/risks for you.
If a colonoscopy can’t be completed safely due to anatomy/angulation, the next step may be a barium enema or other imaging. Dr. Shelby will guide you.
Virtual colonoscopy/CT colonography can be an alternative in select cases (sometimes cash-pay). Please discuss with the doctor.
Barrett’s esophagus typically requires surveillance endoscopy on an interval determined by your findings. Please schedule follow-up to confirm your plan.
Endoscopy follow-up intervals depend on symptoms and findings (e.g., reflux/Barrett’s). If you’re symptom-free, timing may be flexible—confirm with Dr. Shelby.
Endoscopy is very unlikely to permanently affect your gag reflex. Sedation prevents gagging during the procedure.
For endoscopy under anesthesia, you need a ride home. The driver typically doesn’t have to stay—you can call them when you’re ready.
Hiatal hernia can weaken the valve mechanism and allow reflux. Endoscopy helps assess severity and guide treatment.
Hiatal hernia appearance can vary depending on muscle contraction and positioning; mild size differences can be subjective unless very large.
Severe reflux can irritate sensitive nerve endings. Dr. Shelby may use Pepcid/Carafate as boosters and consider endoscopy if not improving.
If ulcer is suspected, Dr. Shelby may treat with pantoprazole plus Carafate and only scope if symptoms persist.
Endoscopy without sedation is not the default. If you want no sedation, it must be explicitly arranged and documented—please speak with the scheduler/doctor first.
If you require a female endoscopist for religious reasons, we can discuss referral options to a female GI provider. Scheduling may take longer at alternate sites.
After the procedure, patients must be driven home and should not drive, fly, or exercise heavily for the rest of the day. Normal activities and diet may be resumed after 24 hours.
After the procedure, patients must be driven home and should not drive, fly, or exercise heavily for the rest of the day. Normal activities and diet may be resumed after 24 hours.

After a colonoscopy or endoscopy, most patients should take it easy for the rest of the day, especially if sedation was used. Avoid driving, alcohol, or strenuous exercise until the next day, or until your discharge instructions say it’s okay. If you had biopsies or a polyp removed, follow your discharge instructions. If you’re unsure, please call the office.
Laboratory testing is performed through LabCorp. Imaging studies are done at San Ramon Regional Medical Center. Pathology services are provided by Precision Pathology or Pathology Services.
